text: Solar production of hydrogen peroxide as a solar fuel from water and dioxygen was achieved using a water oxidation catalyst and a photocatalyst for the two-electron reduction of dioxygen to produce H2O2 in acidic aqueous solutions. Here we report that photocatalytic production of H2O2 was achieved under photoirradiation with the visible light of a Ru(II) complex, [RuII(Me2phen)3)]2+ (Me2phen = 4,7-dimethyl-1,10-phenanthroline) using the solar energy while an Ir(OH)3 was used as a water oxidation catalyst in an O2-saturated aqueous solution of H2SO4. Photoinduced reduction of dioxygen proceeded via electron transfer to the excited state of the [RuII(Me2phen)3]2+ to produce the Ru(III) species [RuIII(Me2phen)3]3+ along with the superoxide radical anion (O2˙−) of which the protonation and subsequent disproportionation of HO2˙ leads to H2O2. Formation of O2 took place through oxidation of water by [RuIII(Me2phen)3]3+ with the aid of Ir(OH)3 catalyst. The photocatalytic reactivity of production of H2O2 was enhanced in the presence of [CoIII(Cp*)(bpy)(H2O)]2+ in place of Ir(OH)3 catalyst nanoparticles in the presence of Sc(NO3)3 in water to produce 62 mm H2O2 under illumination at room temperature. The optimized quantum yield of production of H2O2 at λ = 450 nm in aqueous solution of H2SO4 was determined using a ferrioxalate actinometer to be 37%. The conversion efficiency of solar energy into chemical energy is calculated to be 0.25%.
